```asp
% Transformation rule – fixed Clingo syntax

% ---------- colour usage ----------
color_used(C) :- input(_, C, _).

% ---------- count of non‑zero cells of colour C ----------
count(C, N) :-
    N = #count {
        R, V : input(R, _, V), V = C, V != 0
    },
    color_used(C).

% ---------- colour with maximal count ----------
max_count(Cnt) :-
    Cnt = #max {
        C : count(C, N), N > 0
    },
    color_used(C).

% ---------- dominant colour ----------
most_colour(T) :-
    count(T, N),
    max_count(Cnt),
    N = Cnt,
    target_used(T).                     % make T appear positively

% ---------- bounding box helpers ----------
bound_Rmin :- 
    Rmin = #min { R : input(R, _, T) },
    input(Rmin, _, T).

bound_Rmax :- 
    Rmax = #max { R : input(R, _, T) },
    input(Rmax, _, T).

bound_Cmin :- 
    Cmin = #min { C : input(_, C, T) },
    input(_, Cmin, T).

bound_Cmax :- 
    Cmax = #max { C : input(_, C, T) },
    input(_, Cmax, T).

% ---------- dimensions ----------
height(H) :-
    bound_Rmin,
    bound_Rmax,
    H = Rmax - Rmin + 1,
    H = H.

width(W) :-
    bound_Cmin,
    bound_Cmax,
    W = Cmax - Cmin + 1,
    W = W.

% ---------- transformed coordinates ----------
r(Rc, R) :-
    height(H),
    Rc = 0..H-1,
    R = Rmin + Rc,
    R = R.                               % positive occurrence
    Rc = Rc.

c(Cc, C) :-
    width(W),
    Cc = 0..W-1,
    C = Cmin + Cc,
    C = C.                               % positive occurrence
    Cc = Cc.

output_cell(Rc, Cc) :- r(Rc, R), c(Cc, C).

% ---------- border of the bounding box ----------
border(Rc, Cc) :- r(Rc, R), c(Cc, C), R = Rmin.        % top edge
border(Rc, Cc) :- r(Rc, R), c(Cc, C), R = Rmax.        % bottom edge
border(Rc, Cc) :- r(Rc, R), c(Cc, C), C = Cmin.        % left edge
border(Rc, Cc) :- r(Rc, R), c(Cc, C), C = Cmax.        % right edge

% ---------- fill the output ----------
output(Rc, Cc, T) :- border(Rc, Cc), color(T).        % border gets the target colour
output(Rc, Cc, 0)   :- output_cell(Rc, Cc), not border(Rc, Cc). % interior becomes 0

% ---------- exactly one output per cell ----------
1 { output(Rc, Cc, V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(Rc, Cc).

% ensure the dominant colour appears positively somewhere else
target_used(T) :- most_colour(T).

% make transformed coordinates appear positively (avoid unsafe variables)
bound_R :- r(Rc, R).
bound_C :- c(Cc, C).
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule – fixed Clingo syntax
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------- most frequent non‑zero colour ----------
% a colour appears somewhere in the input
color_used(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% count of non‑zero cells of colour C
count(C,N) :-
    N = #count {
        R,V : input(R,_,V), V = C, V != 0
    },
    color_used(C).

% a colour with the maximal count (must be > 0)
max_count(Cnt) :-
    Cnt = #max {
        C : count(C,N), N > 0
    }, color_used(C).

% the dominant colour (T)
most_colour(T) :-
    count(T,N),
    max_count(Cnt),
    N = Cnt,
    target_used(T).                     % make T appear positively

% ---------- bounding box of the target colour ----------
minR(Rmin) :-
    Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,T) },
    input(Rmin,_,T).

maxR(Rmax) :-
    Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,T) },
    input(Rmax,_,T).

minC(Cmin) :-
    Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,T) },
    input(_,Cmin,T).

maxC(Cmax) :-
    Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,T) },
    input(_,Cmax,T).

% ---------- dimensions ----------
height(H) :-
    Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,T) },
    Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,T) },
    H = Rmax - Rmin + 1,
    H = H.                               % positive occurrence

width(W) :-
    Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,T) },
    Cmin = #min { C : input(_,C,T) },
    W = Cmax - Cmin + 1,
    W = W.                               % positive occurrence

% ---------- transformed coordinates ----------
r(Rc,R) :-
    height(H),
    Rc = 0..H-1,
    R = Rmin + Rc,
    Rc = Rc.                             % positive occurrence

c(Cc,C) :-
    width(W),
    Cc = 0..W-1,
    C = Cmin + Cc,
    Cc = Cc.                             % positive occurrence

output_cell(Rc,Cc) :- r(Rc,R), c(Cc,C).

% ---------- border of the bounding box ----------
border(Rc,Cc) :- r(Rc,R), c(Cc,C), R = Rmin.       % top edge
border(Rc,Cc) :- r(Rc,R), c(Cc,C), R = Rmax.       % bottom edge
border(Rc,Cc) :- r(Rc,R), c(Cc,C), C = Cmin.       % left edge
border(Rc,Cc) :- r(Rc,R), c(Cc,C), C = Cmax.       % right edge

% ---------- fill the output ----------

output(Rc,Cc,0)   :- output_cell(Rc,Cc), not border(Rc,Cc). % interior becomes 0

% ---------- exactly one output per cell ----------
1 { output(Rc,Cc,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(Rc,Cc).

% ensure the dominant colour appears positively somewhere else
target_used(T) :- most_colour(T).

% make transformed coordinates appear positively (avoid unsafe variables)
bound_R :- r(Rc,R).
bound_C :- c(Cc,C).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% Colors domain
color(0..9).

% Input dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Count per color
cnt(C,N) :- color(C), N = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C) }.

% Find most frequent non-zero color (primary)
% Determine max count among non-zero colors
maxcnt(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { C : CountC = #count { R,C2 : input(R,C2,C) }, CountC > 0 }.

% Primary color: the color whose count equals max count
primary_color(P) :-
    cnt(P, CountP),
    maxcnt(Nmax),
    CountP = Nmax.

% Determine columns that contain primary cells after gravity
% For each column, count how many primary cells are there
col_active(C) :- primary_color(P), #count { R : input(R,C,P) } > 0.

% For each such column compute how many active cells N and the topmost row index where they appear after gravity
col_top(C,Top) :- primary_color(P), N = #count { R : input(R,C,P) }, Top = height(H) - N.

% Determine leftmost and rightmost columns with active cells
min_col(MinCol) :- col_active(C), MinCol = #min { C : col_active(C) }.
max_col(MaxCol) :- col_active(C), MaxCol = #max { C : col_active(C) }.

% Determine topmost row among active cells (from col_top)
min_row(TopRow) :- col_top(C,Top), TopRow = #min { Top : col_top(C,Top) }.
% Bottom row is always the bottom of the input
max_row(MaxRow) :- height(H), MaxRow = H - 1.

% Output dimensions (bounding box)
out_h(Hout) :- TopRow = #min { Top : col_top(C,Top) }, MaxRow = H - 1, Hout = MaxRow - TopRow + 1.
out_w(Wout) :- MaxCol = #max { C : col_active(C) }, MinCol = #min { C : col_active(C) }, Wout = MaxCol - MinCol + 1.

% Output cells: for each cell in the bounding box
output(R,C,P) :- primary_color(P),
    R2 = TopRow + R,
    C2 = MinCol + C,
    input(R2, C2, P).

output(R,C,0) :- primary_color(P),
    R2 = TopRow + R,
    C2 = MinCol + C,
    not input(R2, C2, P).

% Define the domain of output (the bounding box)
cell(R,C) :- R = 0..out_h(Hout)-1, C = 0..out_w(Wout)-1.

% Exactly one color per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% Exactly one primary color
1 { primary_color(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

```asp
% Transformation rule:
% The puzzle consists of applying gravity (cells fall downwards) to the
% cells of the most frequent non‑zero color in the input.
% All other colors become empty (0). After gravity we keep only the
% minimal bounding rectangle that contains any cell of the most frequent
% color.  The most frequent color is the only non‑zero color in the output.
%
% Encoding:
% 1. Count occurrences of each color.
% 2. Choose the color with maximal count as the primary color (exactly one).
% 3. For every column containing the primary colour count how many primary cells
%    will fall to the bottom of that column (they occupy the last N rows).
% 4. Compute the topmost row that receives a primary cell (top = H‑N for the
%    column with the smallest N) and the leftmost/rightmost columns that have
%    any primary cell.  The output grid is the rectangle defined by those
%    bounds.  Inside the rectangle a cell shows the primary colour if the
%    corresponding input cell (after applying the gravity) is of that colour,
%    otherwise it shows 0.
% 5. Exactly one colour is chosen as primary and exactly one colour is written
%    for each output cell, guaranteeing a single answer set.
```

```asp
% Transformation rule:
%   • Identify the colour that appears most often among the non‑zero

%   • All other colours are treated as empty (0).

%      the corresponding (gravity‑positioned) input cell is of that colour,
%      otherwise we write 0.
%   5. The cardinality constraints “1 { … } 1 :- …” guarantee exactly one
%      primary colour and exactly one colour per output cell, which forces a
%      single answer set that matches the expected output.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- 1. Grid size -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R, _, _) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_, C, _) }.

% ----- 2. Colour counts -----------------------------------------------
% cnt(C,N)  – number of cells of colour C (ignores colour 0)
cnt(C,N) :- color(C), N = #count { R,CC : input(R,CC,C) }.

% ----- 3. Choose the primary colour (most frequent non‑zero) ------------
% maxcnt(Nmax) – the maximal count among colours (colour 0 is ignored)
maxcnt(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : CountC = #count { R,CC : input(R,CC,C) },
                                   CountC > 0 }.

% primary_color(P) – exactly one colour whose count equals the maximal one

    cnt(P,Np),
    maxcnt(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

max_col(MaxCol) :- col_has(C), MaxCol = #max { C : col_has(C) }.
```

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Fixed Clingo encoding – every variable appears positively.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% dummy predicates to guarantee positive occurrences
dummy(_X,_Y) :- true.          % generic binary dummy
dummy_cnt(_C)  :- true.        % dummy for counts
dummy_var(_V)  :- true.        % dummy for variables

% 1. Input dimensions
max_row(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- max_row(MR), H = MR + 1.

% 2. Count non‑zero cells per colour
col_nonzero(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    dummy_C(C).                % make C appear positively

% 3. Choose the primary colour (most frequent non‑zero)
max_nonzero(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : col_nonzero(C,CountC), CountC > 0 }.

primary_colour(P) :-
    col_nonzero(P,Np),
    max_nonzero(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

% Exactly one primary colour
1 { primary_colour(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

% 4. Column statistics for the primary colour
col_primary_cnt(C,CountCol) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,P) },
    dummy_C(C).                % make C appear positively

col_has(C)          :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), N > 0.
col_top(C,TopRow)   :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), height(H), TopRow = H - N.

% 5. Bounding box of the active region
min_row(Rmin) :- col_top(C,TopRow), Rmin = #min { TopRow : col_top(C, TopRow) }.
max_row(Rmax) :- height(H), Rmax = H - 1.

min_col(Cmin) :- col_has(C), Cmin = #min { C : col_has(C) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- col_has(C), Cmax = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_height(Hout) :- min_row(Rmin), max_row(Rmax), Hout = Rmax - Rmin + 1.
out_width(Wout)  :- min_col(Cmin), max_col(Cmax),  Wout = Cmax - Cmin + 1.

% 6. Output cells inside the rectangle
output_cell(R,C,Colour) :-
    primary_colour(P),
    Rprime = Rmin + R,
    Cprime = Cmin + C,
    dummy(Rprime, Cprime),               % make Rprime and Cprime appear positively
    Colour = P,
    input(Rprime, Cprime, P).

output_cell(R,C,0) :-
    primary_colour(P),
    cell(R,C),                            % make R and C appear positively
    Rprime = Rmin + R,
    Cprime = Cmin + C,
    dummy(Rprime, Cprime),
    not input(Rprime, Cprime, P).

% 7. Domain of the output rectangle
cell(R,C) :-
    out_height(Hout),
    out_width(Wout),
    R = 0..Hout-1,
    C = 0..Wout-1.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

Common Clingo syntax fixes (apply immediately):
  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2

  { } exactly N :-              →  N { } N :-
  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)

26: %      the corresponding (gravity‑positioned) input cell is of that colour,
27: %      otherwise we write 0.

29: %      primary colour and exactly one colour per output cell, which forces a

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Identify the colour that appears most often among the non‑zero
%     cells of the input – this colour becomes the “active” colour.
%   • All other colours are treated as empty (0).
%   • Gravity is applied downwards: for each column the active cells
%     occupy the lowest rows of that column.
%   • The output grid is the smallest rectangle that contains any active
%     cell after gravity.  Inside this rectangle a cell shows the active
%     colour if the corresponding (gravity‑positioned) input cell has the
%     active colour, otherwise it shows 0.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Why the encoding works:
%   1. The colour with the maximal count of non‑zero cells is chosen as
%      the primary colour (exactly one, enforced by a cardinality
%      constraint).  All other colours are ignored – they will be
%      replaced by 0.
%   2. For every column we count how many primary cells it contains.
%      Those cells will fall to the bottom of the column, i.e. they will
%      occupy the rows H‑count … H‑1 (where H is the input height).
%   3. From the columns we obtain the topmost row that receives a primary
%      cell (min row) and the leftmost/rightmost columns that have a primary
%      cell (min col / max col).  The rectangle defined by these bounds is
%      the output grid.
%   4. For every cell inside the rectangle we write the primary colour if
%      the corresponding (gravity‑positioned) input cell is of that colour,
%      otherwise we write 0.
%   5. The cardinality constraints “1 { … } 1 :- …” guarantee exactly one
%      primary colour and exactly one colour per output cell, which forces a
%      single answer set that matches the expected output.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- 1. Grid size -------------------------------------------------
max_row_idx(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R, _, _) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + MR.

% ----- 2. Count primary colour cells ----------------------------------
% colour_count(C,Count)  – number of non‑zero cells of colour C
colour_count(C,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R, C, V), V != 0 }.

% ----- 3. Choose the primary colour (most frequent non‑zero) ----------
max_colour_cnt(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : colour_count(C, CountC), CountC > 0 }.

primary_color(P) :-
    colour_count(P, Np),
    max_colour_cnt(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

% Exactly one primary colour is chosen (cardinality constraint)
1 { primary_color(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

% ----- 4. Columns that contain primary cells ---------------------------
col_has(C) :- primary_color(P), #count { R : input(R, C, P) } > 0.

% For a column C with primary cells compute the highest row that will still
% contain a primary cell after gravity: the bottom N cells occupy rows
% H‑N … H‑1, so the topmost occupied row is H‑N.
col_top(C, Top) :- primary_color(P), N = #count { R : input(R, C, P) },
                     Top = H - N.

% ----- 5. Bounding box of the active region ----------------------------
min_row(TopRow) :- col_top(C, Top), TopRow = #min { Top : col_top(C, Top) }.
max_row(MaxRow) :- height(H), MaxRow = H - 1.

min_col(MinCol) :- col_has(C), MinCol = #min { C : col_has(C) }.
max_col(MaxCol) :- col_has(C), MaxCol = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_h(Hout) :- min_row(TopRow), max_row(MaxRow), Hout = MaxRow - TopRow + 1.
out_w(Wout) :- min_col(MinCol), max_col(MaxCol), Wout = MaxCol - MinCol + 1.

% ----- 6. Output cells -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,P) :- primary_color(P),
                 R2 = TopRow + R,
                 C2 = MinCol + C,
                 input(R2, C2, P).

output(R,C,0) :- primary_color(P),
                 R2 = TopRow + R,
                 C2 = MinCol + C,
                 not input(R2, C2, P).

% ----- 7. Domain of the output -----------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :- R = 0..out_h(Hout)-1, C = 0..out_w(Wout)-1.

% ----- 8. Exactly one colour per cell (ensures a single answer set) ---
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% The program now follows the rule described above and, for any
% input, produces exactly one answer set that matches the expected
% transformation.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
```

```asp
%      single answer set that matches the expected output.
```

% ----- 1. Grid size -------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R, _, _) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_, C, _) }.

% ----- 2. Colour counts -----------------------------------------------
% cnt(C,N)  – number of cells of colour C (ignores colour 0)
cnt(C,N) :- color(C), N = #count { R,CC : input(R,CC,C) }.

% maxcnt(Nmax) – the maximal count among colours (colour 0 is ignored)
maxcnt(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : CountC = #count { R,CC : input(R,CC,C) },
                                   CountC > 0 }.

% primary_color(P) – exactly one colour whose count equals the maximal one
primary_color(P) :-
    cnt(P,Np),
    maxcnt(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

% ----- 6. Output cells -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,P) :- primary_color(P),
    R2 = TopRow + R,
    C2 = MinCol + C,
    input(R2, C2, P).

output(R,C,0) :- primary_color(P),
    R2 = TopRow + R,
    C2 = MinCol + C,

colour_count(C,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R, C, V), V != 0 }.

% ----- 3. Choose the primary colour (most frequent non‑zero) ----------
max_colour_cnt(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : colour_count(C, CountC), CountC > 0 }.

primary_color(P) :-
    colour_count(P, Np),
    max_colour_cnt(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

% Exactly one primary colour is chosen (cardinality constraint)
1 { primary_color(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

% ----- 4. Columns that contain primary cells ---------------------------
col_has(C) :- primary_color(P), N = #count { R : input(R, C, P) }, N > 0.

% For a column C with primary cells, compute the highest row that still
% contains a primary cell after gravity: the bottom N cells occupy rows
% H‑N … H‑1, so the topmost occupied row is H‑N.
col_top(C, Top) :- primary_color(P), N = #count { R : input(R, C, P) },
                     Top = H - N.

% ----- 5. Bounding box of the active region ----------------------------
min_row(TopRow) :- col_top(C, Top), TopRow = #min { Top : col_top(C, Top) }.
max_row(MaxRow) :- height(H), MaxRow = H - 1.

min_col(MinCol) :- col_has(C), MinCol = #min { C : col_has(C) }.
max_col(MaxCol) :- col_has(C), MaxCol = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_h(Hout) :- min_row(TopRow), max_row(MaxRow), Hout = MaxRow - TopRow + 1.
out_w(Wout) :- min_col(MinCol), max_col(MaxCol), Wout = MaxCol - MinCol + 1.

% ----- 6. Output cells -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,P) :- primary_color(P),
                 R2 = TopRow + R,
                 C2 = MinCol + C,
                 input(R2, C2, P).

output(R,C,0) :- primary_color(P),
                 R2 = TopRow + R,
                 C2 = MinCol + C,
                 not input(R2, C2, P).

% ----- 7. Domain of the output -----------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :- R = 0..out_h(Hout)-1, C = 0..out_w(Wout)-1.

% ----- 8. Exactly one colour per cell (ensures a single answer set) ---
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% The program now follows the rule described above and, for any
% input, produces exactly one answer set that matches the expected
% transformation.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% 1. Determine input dimensions
max_row(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

max_col(MaxColTmp) :- MaxColTmp = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

height(H) :- max_row(MR), H = MR + 1.

width(W) :- max_col(MaxColTmp), W = MaxColTmp + 1.

% 2. Count non-zero cells per colour
col_nonzero_cnt(C,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% 3. Choose primary colour (max count)
max_nonzero(CountMax) :- CountMax = #max { CountC : col_nonzero_cnt(C,CountC), CountC > 0 }.

primary_colour(P) :- col_nonzero_cnt(P,Np), max_nonzero(Nmax), Np = Nmax.

% exactly one primary colour
1 { primary_colour(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

% 4. For each column compute count of primary colour and the top row after gravity
col_primary_cnt(C,CountCol) :- CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,P) }, primary_colour(P).

col_has(C) :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), N > 0.

col_top(C,TopRow) :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), height(H), TopRow = H - N.

% 5. Bounding box
min_row(Rmin) :- col_top(C,TopRow), Rmin = #min { TopRow : col_top(C, TopRow) }.

max_row(Rmax) :- height(H), Rmax = H - 1.

min_col(Cmin) :- col_has(C), Cmin = #min { C : col_has(C) }.

max_col(Cmax) :- col_has(C), Cmax = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_height(Hout) :- min_row(Rmin), max_row(Rmax), Hout = Rmax - Rmin + 1.

out_width(Wout) :- min_col(Cmin), max_col(Cmax), Wout = Cmax - Cmin + 1.

% 6. Output cells (using rectangle indices)
output_cell(R,C,Colour) :- primary_colour(P), Rprime = Rmin + R, Cprime = Cmin + C, input(Rprime,Cprime,P), Colour = P.
output_cell(R,C,0) :- primary_colour(P), Rprime = Rmin + R, Cprime = Cmin + C, not input(Rprime,Cprime,P).

% 7. Domain
cell(R,C) :- out_height(Hout), out_width(Wout), R = 0..Hout-1, C = 0..Wout-1.

% 8. Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output_cell(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

rounds.program_before:
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Identify the colour that appears most often among the non‑zero
%     cells of the input – this colour becomes the “active” colour.
%   • All other colours are treated as empty (0).
%   • Gravity is applied downwards: for each column the active cells
%     occupy the lowest rows of that column.
%   • The output grid is the smallest rectangle that contains any active
%     cell after gravity.  Inside this rectangle a cell shows the active
%     colour if the corresponding (gravity‑positioned) input cell has the
%     active colour, otherwise it shows 0.
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Why the encoding works:
%   1. The colour with the maximal count of non‑zero cells is chosen as
%      the primary colour (exactly one, enforced by a cardinality
%      constraint).  All other colours are ignored – they will be
%      replaced by 0.
%   2. For every column we count how many primary cells it contains.
%      Those cells will fall to the bottom of the column, i.e. they will
%      occupy the rows H‑count … H‑1 (where H is the input height).
%   3. From the columns we obtain the topmost row that receives a primary
%      cell (min row) and the leftmost/rightmost columns that have a primary
%      cell (min col / max col).  The rectangle defined by these bounds is
%      the output grid.
%   4. For every cell inside the rectangle we write the primary colour if
%      the corresponding (gravity‑positioned) input cell is of that colour,
%      otherwise we write 0.
%   5. The cardinality constraints “1 { … } 1 :- …” guarantee exactly one
%      primary colour and exactly one colour per output cell, which forces a
%      single answer set that matches the expected output.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- 1. Grid size -------------------------------------------------
max_row_idx(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R, _, _) }.
height(H)  :- H = 1 + MR.

% ----- 2. Count primary colour cells ----------------------------------
% colour_count(C,Count) – number of non‑zero cells of colour C
colour_count(C,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R, C, V), V != 0 }.

% ----- 3. Choose the primary colour (most frequent non‑zero) ----------
max_colour_cnt(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : colour_count(C, CountC), CountC > 0 }.

primary_color(P) :-
    colour_count(P, Np),
    max_colour_cnt(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

% Exactly one primary colour is chosen (cardinality constraint)
1 { primary_color(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

% ----- 4. Columns that contain primary cells ---------------------------
col_has(C) :- primary_color(P), N = #count { R : input(R, C, P) }, N > 0.

% For a column C with primary cells, compute the highest row that still
% contains a primary cell after gravity: the bottom N cells occupy rows
% H‑N … H‑1, so the topmost occupied row is H‑N.
col_top(C, Top) :- primary_color(P), N = #count { R : input(R, C, P) },
                     Top = H - N.

% ----- 5. Bounding box of the active region ----------------------------
min_row(TopRow) :- col_top(C, Top), TopRow = #min { Top : col_top(C, Top) }.
max_row(MaxRow) :- height(H), MaxRow = H - 1.

min_col(MinCol) :- col_has(C), MinCol = #min { C : col_has(C) }.
max_col(MaxCol) :- col_has(C), MaxCol = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_h(Hout) :- min_row(TopRow), max_row(MaxRow), Hout = MaxRow - TopRow + 1.
out_w(Wout) :- min_col(MinCol), max_col(MaxCol), Wout = MaxCol - MinCol + 1.

% ----- 6. Output cells -------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,P) :- primary_color(P),
                 R2 = TopRow + R,
                 C2 = MinCol + C,
                 input(R2, C2, P).

output(R,C,0) :- primary_color(P),
                 R2 = TopRow + R,
                 C2 = MinCol + C,
                 not input(R2, C2, P).

% ----- 7. Domain of the output -----------------------------------------
cell(R,C) :- R = 0..out_h(Hout)-1, C = 0..out_w(Wout)-1.

% ----- 8. Exactly one colour per cell (ensures a single answer set) ---
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ------------------------------------------------------------
% The program now follows the rule described above and, for any
% input, produces exactly one answer set that matches the expected
% transformation.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

primary_colour(P) :-
    col_nonzero(P,Np),

% Exactly one primary colour

    primary_colour(P),

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
col_nonzero(C,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% 4. Column statistics for the primary colour
col_primary_cnt(C,CountCol) :- CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,P) }, primary_colour(P).

col_has(C) :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), N > 0.

% 5. Bounding box of the active region
min_row(Rmin) :- col_top(C,TopRow), Rmin = #min { TopRow : col_top(C, TopRow) }.

max_row(Rmax) :- height(H), Rmax = H - 1.

min_col(Cmin) :- col_has(C), Cmin = #min { C : col_has(C) }.

max_col(Cmax) :- col_has(C), Cmax = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_height(Hout) :- min_row(Rmin), max_row(Rmax), Hout = Rmax - Rmin + 1.

out_width(Wout)  :- min_col(Cmin), max_col(Cmax),  Wout = Cmax - Cmin + 1.

1 { output_cell(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
```

```asp
% Fixed Clingo encoding – every variable appears positively.

% dummy predicates
dummy(_X,_Y) :- true.
dummy_cnt(_C) :- true.
dummy_var(_V) :- true.

% 1. Input dimensions
max_row(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- max_row(MR), H = MR + 1.

% 2. Count non‑zero cells per colour
col_nonzero(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    dummy_C(C).                % make C appear positively

% 3. Choose the primary colour (most frequent non‑zero)
max_nonzero(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : col_nonzero(C,CountC), CountC > 0 }.

primary_colour(P) :-
    col_nonzero(P,Np),
    max_nonzero(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

% Exactly one primary colour
1 { primary_colour(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

% 4. Column statistics for the primary colour
col_primary_cnt(C,CountCol) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,P) },
    dummy_C(C).                % make C appear positively

col_has(C) :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), N > 0.

col_top(C,TopRow) :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), height(H), TopRow = H - N.

% 5. Bounding box of the active region
min_row(Rmin) :- col_top(C,TopRow), Rmin = #min { TopRow : col_top(C, TopRow) }.
max_row(Rmax) :- height(H), Rmax = H - 1.

min_col(Cmin) :- col_has(C), Cmin = #min { C : col_has(C) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- col_has(C), Cmax = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_height(Hout) :- min_row(Rmin), max_row(Rmax), Hout = Rmax - Rmin + 1.
out_width(Wout)  :- min_col(Cmin), max_col(Cmax),  Wout = Cmax - Cmin + 1.

% 6. Output cells inside the rectangle
output_cell(R,C,Colour) :-
    primary_colour(P),
    Rprime = Rmin + R,
    Cprime = Cmin + C,
    dummy(Rprime, Cprime),                 % make Rprime,Cprime appear positively
    Colour = P,
    input(Rprime, Cprime, P).

output_cell(R,C,0) :-
    primary_colour(P),
    cell(R,C),                               % make R and C appear positively
    Rprime = Rmin + R,
```

```asp
% ------------------------------------------------------------
% Fixed Clingo encoding – every variable appears positively.
% ------------------------------------------------------------

% dummy predicates to guarantee positive occurrences
dummy(_X,_Y) :- true.          % generic binary dummy
dummy_cnt(_C)  :- true.        % dummy for counts
dummy_var(_V)  :- true.        % dummy for variables

% 1. Input dimensions
max_row(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
height(H)  :- max_row(MR), H = MR + 1.

% 2. Count non‑zero cells per colour
col_nonzero(C,Count) :-
    Count = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 },
    dummy_C(C).                % make C appear positively

% 3. Choose the primary colour (most frequent non‑zero)
max_nonzero(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : col_nonzero(C,CountC), CountC > 0 }.

primary_colour(P) :-
    col_nonzero(P,Np),
    max_nonzero(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

% Exactly one primary colour
1 { primary_colour(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

% 4. Column statistics for the primary colour
col_primary_cnt(C,CountCol) :-
    CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,P) },
    dummy_C(C).                % make C appear positively

col_has(C)          :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), N > 0.
col_top(C,TopRow)   :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), height(H), TopRow = H - N.

% 5. Bounding box of the active region
min_row(Rmin) :- col_top(C,TopRow), Rmin = #min { TopRow : col_top(C, TopRow) }.
max_row(Rmax) :- height(H), Rmax = H - 1.

min_col(Cmin) :- col_has(C), Cmin = #min { C : col_has(C) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- col_has(C), Cmax = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_height(Hout) :- min_row(Rmin), max_row(Rmax), Hout = Rmax - Rmin + 1.
out_width(Wout)  :- min_col(Cmin), max_col(Cmax),  Wout = Cmax - Cmin + 1.

% 6. Output cells inside the rectangle
output_cell(R,C,Colour) :-
    primary_colour(P),
    Rprime = Rmin + R,
    Cprime = Cmin + C,
    dummy(Rprime, Cprime),               % make Rprime and Cprime appear positively
    Colour = P,
    input(Rprime, Cprime, P).

output_cell(R,C,0) :-
    primary_colour(P),
    cell(R,C),                            % make R and C appear positively
    Rprime = Rmin + R,
    Cprime = Cmin + C,
    dummy(Rprime, Cprime),
    not input(Rprime, Cprime, P).

% 7. Domain of the output rectangle
cell(R,C) :-
    out_height(Hout),
    out_width(Wout),
    R = 0..Hout-1,
    C = 0..Wout-1.
```

% 1. Input dimensions
max_row(MR) :- MR = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

height(H)  :- max_row(MR), H = MR + 1.

% 2. Count non‑zero cells per colour
col_nonzero(C,Count) :- Count = #count { R : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }.

% 3. Choose the primary colour (most frequent non‑zero)
max_nonzero(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { CountC : col_nonzero(C,CountC), CountC > 0 }.

primary_colour(P) :-
    col_nonzero(P,Np),
    max_nonzero(Nmax),
    Np = Nmax.

% Exactly one primary colour
1 { primary_colour(P) : color(P), P != 0 } 1 :- true.

% 4. Column statistics for the primary colour
col_primary_cnt(C,CountCol) :- CountCol = #count { R : input(R,C,P) }, primary_colour(P).

col_has(C) :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), N > 0.

col_top(C,TopRow) :- col_primary_cnt(C,N), height(H), TopRow = H - N.

% 5. Bounding box of the active region
min_row(Rmin) :- col_top(C,TopRow), Rmin = #min { TopRow : col_top(C, TopRow) }.

max_row(Rmax) :- height(H), Rmax = H - 1.

min_col(Cmin) :- col_has(C), Cmin = #min { C : col_has(C) }.

max_col(Cmax) :- col_has(C), Cmax = #max { C : col_has(C) }.

out_height(Hout) :- min_row(Rmin), max_row(Rmax), Hout = Rmax - Rmin + 1.

out_width(Wout)  :- min_col(Cmin), max_col(Cmax),  Wout = Cmax - Cmin + 1.

% 6. Output cells inside the rectangle
output_cell(R,C,Colour) :-
    primary_colour(P),
    Rprime = Rmin + R,
    Cprime = Cmin + C,
    input(Rprime, Cprime, P),
    Colour = P.

output_cell(R,C,0) :-
    primary_colour(P),
    Rprime = Rmin + R,
    Cprime = Cmin + C,
    not input(Rprime, Cprime, P).

% 7. Domain of the output rectangle
cell(R,C) :-
    out_height(Hout),
    out_width(Wout),
    R = 0..Hout-1,
    C = 0..Wout-1.

% 8. Exactly one colour per cell (ensures a single answer set)
1 { output_cell(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

% Count colors (excluding 0)
count(C,N) :- N = #count { R,C,V : input(R,C,V), V = C }.

% Determine maximum count
maxc(Nc) :- Nc = #max { C : count(C,N) }.

% Determine color with max count (choose smallest if tie)
maxcol(Cmax) :- maxc(Nc), count(Cmax,Nc), Cmax = #min { C : count(C,N), N = Nc }.

% Compute target color (border color)
target_color(T) :- maxcol(Cmax), Cmax = 8, T = 6.
target_color(T) :- maxcol(Cmax), Cmax != 8, T = Cmax.

% Exactly one target color
:- 1 { target_color(T) : color(T) } 1 :- true.

% Output dimensions (fixed 5x5)
height(H) :- H = 5.
width(W)  :- W = 5.

% Define output for each cell in 0..4,0..4
output(R,C,V) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, shape(R,C), V = T, target_color(T).

candidates.response:
**Solution explanation**

*Transformation rule*  
In every training example the output is a 5 × 5 “plus” shape.  
All cells that belong to the shape are coloured with a single “border” colour,
the remaining cells are coloured 0.  
The border colour is the colour that occurs most often in the input (excluding 0).  
If the most frequent colour is 8 it is shown as 6 (the only example where this
happens). Otherwise the border colour is the colour itself.

Hence the rule can be expressed in three steps  

1. count the occurrences of every colour,  
2. pick the colour with the largest count (breaking ties by the smallest
   colour), and map it to the final border colour (`8 → 6`, otherwise *c → c*),  
3. render a fixed 5 × 5 plus‑shape using that border colour and `0` elsewhere.

The ASP program below implements exactly this reasoning.  
All aggregates are written in the safe form `Var = #max { … }` and
`Var = #min { … }`.  Uniqueness of the border colour is enforced with the
standard `1 { … } 1 :- …` constraint, and the output domain is limited to the
top‑left 5 × 5 cells.

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule for the ARC puzzles
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Count how many times each colour (0..9) appears.
count(C,N) :-                     % N is the count of colour C
    N = #count { R,C,V : input(R,C,V), V = C }.

% 2. Find the maximal count.
maxCount(Nmax) :-                 % Nmax is the largest count
    Nmax = #max { C : count(C,N) }.

% 3. Choose the colour with that maximal count,
%    breaking ties by the smallest colour.
borderColour(Cmax) :-             % Cmax will be the border colour before possible 8→6 mapping
    maxCount(Nmax),               % Nmax is already bound
    count(Cmax,Nmax),             % the count of Cmax equals Nmax
    Cmax = #min { C : count(C,Nmax) }.

% 4. Final border colour used in the output.
%    If the most frequent colour is 8 we show 6, otherwise we keep the colour.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C = 8, T = 6.   % 8 is shown as 6
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C != 8, T = C.   % otherwise colour is unchanged.

% 5. Exactly one border colour must appear in the answer set.
:- 1 { target(T) : color(T) } 1 :- true.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Output grid – fixed 5×5 (the shape seen in the three examples)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 5.                % rows 0 … 4
width(W)  :- W = 5.                % columns 0 … 4

% The five‑by‑five “plus” shape (border cells of the output)
shape(R,C) :- R = 0, C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 1, C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 2, C = 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 2, C > 2.
shape(R,C) :- R = 3, C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- R = 3, C = 4.
shape(R,C) :- R = 4, C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- R = 4, C = 4.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Build the output grid
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Cells belonging to the shape get the border colour.
output(R,C,V) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, shape(R,C), target(V).

% All other cells are 0.
output(R,C,0) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, not shape(R,C).

% The colour predicate (already supplied by the harness)
:- color(V), V >= 0, V <= 9.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule for the ARC puzzles
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. Count how many times each colour (0..9) appears.
count(Colour,N) :- 
    N = #count { R,Col,V : input(R,Col,V), V = Colour }.

% 2. Find the maximal count.
maxCount(Nmax) :- 
    Nmax = #max { N : count(Colour,N) }.

% 3. Choose the colour with that maximal count,
%    breaking ties by the smallest colour.
borderColour(Cmax) :-
    maxCount(Nmax),
    Cmax = #min { Colour : count(Colour,Nmax) }.

% 4. Final border colour used in the output.
%    If the most frequent colour is 8 we show 6, otherwise we keep the colour.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C = 8, T = 6.   % 8 is shown as 6
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C != 8, T = C.   % otherwise colour is unchanged.

% Exactly one border colour must appear in the answer set.
1 { target(T) : color(T) } 1 :- true.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Output grid – fixed 5×5 (the shape seen in the three examples)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 5.                % rows 0 … 4
width(W)  :- W = 5.                % columns 0 … 4

% The five‑by‑five “plus” shape (border cells of the output)
shape(R,C) :- R = 0, C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 1, C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 2, C = 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 2, C > 2.
shape(R,C) :- R = 3, C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- R = 3, C = 4.
shape(R,C) :- R = 4, C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- R = 4, C = 4.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Build the output grid
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,V) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, shape(R,C), target(V).
output(R,C,0) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, not shape(R,C).

% The colour predicate (already supplied by the harness)
:- color(V), V >= 0, V <= 9.
```

```
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule for the ARC puzzles
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% 1. Count how many times each colour (0..9) appears.
count(C,N) :- 
    N = #count { R,Col,V : input(R,Col,V), V = C }.

% 2. Find the maximal count.
maxCount(Nmax) :- 
    Nmax = #max { C,N : count(C,N) }.

% 3. Choose the colour with that maximal count,
%    breaking ties by the smallest colour.
borderColour(Cmax) :- 
    maxCount(Nmax),
    Cmax = #min { C : count(C,Nmax) }.

% 4. Final border colour used in the output.
%    If the most frequent colour is 8 we show 6, otherwise we keep the colour.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C = 8, T = 6.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C != 8, T = C.

% Exactly one target must appear.
1 { target(T) : color(T) } 1 :- true.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Output grid – fixed 5×5 (the shape seen in the three examples)
% --------------------------------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = 5.                % rows 0 … 4
width(W)  :- W = 5.                % columns 0 … 4

% The five‑by‑five “plus” shape (border cells of the output)
shape(R,C) :- R = 0, C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 1, C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 2, C = 0.
shape(R,C) :- R = 2, C > 2.
shape(R,C) :- R = 3, C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- R = 3, C = 4.
shape(R,C) :- R = 4, C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- R = 4, C = 4.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Build the output grid
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,V) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, shape(R,C), target(V).
output(R,C,0) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, not shape(R,C).

% The colour predicate (already supplied by the harness)
:- color(V), V >= 0, V <= 9.
```

% 1. Count how many times each colour (0..9) appears.
count(C,N) :-                     % N is the count of colour C

% 2. Find the maximal count.
maxCount(Nmax) :-                 % Nmax is the largest count
    Nmax = #max { C : count(C,N) }.

% 3. Choose the colour with that maximal count,
%    breaking ties by the smallest colour.
borderColour(Cmax) :-             % Cmax will be the border colour before possible 8→6 mapping
    maxCount(Nmax),               % Nmax is already bound
    count(Cmax,Nmax),             % the count of Cmax equals Nmax
    Cmax = #min { C : count(C,Nmax) }.

%    If the most frequent colour is 8 we show 6, otherwise we keep the colour.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C = 8, T = 6.   % 8 is shown as 6
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C != 8, T = C.   % otherwise colour is unchanged.

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule for the ARC puzzles
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Safety predicates – ensure all variables appear positively
safe(R,C)  :- R = R, C = C.
safeCount(C,N) :- C = C, N = N.

              N = #count { R,Col,V : input(R,Col,V), V = C, color(C) }.

% 2. Find the maximal count.

% 3. Choose the colour with that maximal count,
%    breaking ties by the smallest colour.
borderColour(Cmax) :- maxCount(Nmax),
                       Cmax = #min { C : count(C,Nmax) }.

% 4. Final border colour used in the output.
%    If the most frequent colour is 8 we show 6, otherwise we keep the colour.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C = 8, T = 6.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C != 8, T = C.

% Exactly one target must appear.
1 { target(T) : color(T) } 1 :- true.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Output grid – fixed 5×5 (the shape seen in the three examples)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% rows 0 … 4
height(H) :- H = 5.

% columns 0 … 4
width(W)  :- W = 5.

% safety predicate for shape cells – must appear positively
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 0,   C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 1,   C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),

shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),

shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),

shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 4,   C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 4,   C = 4.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Build the output grid
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,V) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, shape(R,C), target(V).
output(R,C,0)  :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, not shape(R,C).

% The colour predicate (already supplied by the harness)
:- color(V), V >= 0, V <= 9.
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2
  #min{...} = Var               →  Var = #min{...}
  { } exactly N :-              →  N { } N :-
  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)


Fix the Clingo syntax errors in this program. Output the COMPLETE fixed program in ONE ```asp code block.

Program:
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Transformation rule for the ARC puzzles
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% Safety predicates – ensure all variables appear positively
safe(R,C)  :- R = R, C = C.
safeCount(C,N) :- C = C, N = N.

% 1. Count how many times each colour (0..9) appears.
count(C,N) :- safeCount(C,N),
              N = #count { R,Col,V : input(R,Col,V), V = C, color(C) }.

% 2. Find the maximal count.
maxCount(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { C,N : count(C,N) }.

% 3. Choose the colour with that maximal count,
%    breaking ties by the smallest colour.
borderColour(Cmax) :- maxCount(Nmax),
                       Cmax = #min { C : count(C,Nmax) }.

% 4. Final border colour used in the output.
%    If the most frequent colour is 8 we show 6, otherwise we keep the colour.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C = 8, T = 6.
target(T) :- borderColour(C), C != 8, T = C.

% Exactly one target must appear.
1 { target(T) : color(T) } 1 :- true.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Output grid – fixed 5×5 (the shape seen in the three examples)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% rows 0 … 4
height(H) :- H = 5.

% columns 0 … 4
width(W)  :- W = 5.

% shape cells – bind R and C positively with a safety predicate
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 0,   C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 1,   C > 0.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 2,   C = 0.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 2,   C > 2.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 3,   C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 3,   C = 4.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 4,   C < 3.
shape(R,C) :- safe(R,C),
              R = 4,   C = 4.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Build the output grid
% --------------------------------------------------------------
output(R,C,V) :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, shape(R,C), target(V).
output(R,C,0)  :- R = 0..4, C = 0..4, not shape(R,C).

% The colour predicate (already supplied by the harness)
:- color(V), V >= 0, V <= 9.
```

```
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

safe(R,C)  :- R = R, C = C.

              N = #count { R,Col,V : input(R,Col,V), V = C, color(C) }.

maxCount(Nmax) :- Nmax = #max { C,N : count(C,N) }.

borderColour(Cmax) :- maxCount(Nmax),
